content.question: To what extent would reversing the gymnázium tracking system (delaying selection to age 16 like Poland 1999, or abolishing it like Finland) reduce ethnic segregation and socioeconomic stratification specifically, versus reducing these inequalities through more targeted interventions (e.g., within-school ability grouping reform, admissions bias correction, funding redistribution)?
content.reduction_path: Comparative case analysis of ethnic and socioeconomic segregation patterns across Hungarian schools grouped by: (a) selective gymnázium presence, (b) free-choice intensity, (c) curriculum differentiation, and (d) funding/resource gaps; paired with microsimulation modeling the effect of tracking-age removal while holding other institutional features constant.
